stem: What is the remainder when \( [[a]]\cdot[[b]] + [[c]] \) is divided by \( [[m]] \)?
solution: Compute the expression, then reduce modulo \( [[m]] \): \( [[a]]\cdot[[b]]+[[c]] \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This reduces the product before adding \( c \), but forgets to take the final modulus of the whole sum.
This applies the wrong order of operations, multiplying \( b \) and \( c \) instead of \( a \) and \( b \).
This reduces each number separately and adds them, without accounting for the multiplication.
This multiplies all three numbers together instead of computing \( ab+c \).
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.

stem: What is the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \) is divided by \( [[m]] \)?
solution: Compute \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \) and reduce modulo \( [[m]] \) (or reduce the base first and reduce again at the end): \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This reduces the base modulo \( m \) first but forgets to reduce AGAIN after raising it to the power.
This multiplies the base and exponent instead of raising the base to that power.
This swaps the roles of the base and the exponent.
This forgets to apply the exponent at all.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.

stem: What is the modular inverse of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[m]] \) — that is, the integer \( x \) with \( 0 \le x < [[m]] \) such that \( [[a]]x \equiv 1 \pmod{[[m]]} \)?
solution: Find \( x \) satisfying \( [[a]]x\equiv 1 \pmod{[[m]]} \) (e.g. via the extended Euclidean algorithm): \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps which number is inverted modulo the other.
This subtracts the correct inverse from the modulus, giving the wrong residue.
This just reports \( a \) itself reduced mod \( m \), not its inverse.
This subtracts \( a \) from the modulus, unrelated to the actual inverse.
This is one more than the correct inverse.
This is one less than the correct inverse.

stem: What is the units (ones) digit of \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \)?
solution: The units digit is the remainder modulo 10, which cycles with a short period: \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{10} \).
distractor_rationales: This just reports the units digit of the BASE, ignoring the effect of raising it to a power.
This computes the last TWO digits (mod 100) instead of just the units digit.
This reports a digit of the exponent, unrelated to the units digit of the power.
This multiplies the base and exponent instead of computing the power.
This is one more than the correct units digit.
This is one less than the correct units digit.

stem: What is the smallest non-negative integer \( x \) satisfying \( [[a]]x \equiv [[b]] \pmod{[[m]]} \)?
solution: Multiply both sides by the modular inverse of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[m]] \): \( x \equiv [[b]]\cdot a^{-1} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( a \) and \( b \) instead of multiplying \( b \) by the inverse of \( a \).
This forgets to multiply by \( b \), reporting only the inverse of \( a \).
This just reports \( b \) reduced mod \( m \), ignoring the coefficient \( a \) entirely.
This subtracts the correct solution from the modulus, giving the wrong residue.
This is one more than the correct solution.
This is one less than the correct solution.

stem: What is the remainder when \( 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + [[n]] \) is divided by \( [[m]] \)?
solution: The sum is \( \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \); reduce it modulo \( [[m]] \): \( \dfrac{[[n]]([[n]]+1)}{2} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 in the sum formula.
This uses \( n^2 \) instead of the correct triangular-number formula \( n(n+1)/2 \).
This reduces \( n \) modulo \( m \) before applying the formula, which does not commute correctly with the division by 2.
This just reports \( n \) reduced mod \( m \), ignoring the summation entirely.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.
